notes: The product will not automatically update the kernel to the latest version as it could cause various unforeseen issues in systems that haven't tested the upgrade first.
Automaticall updating every installed package could cause unforeseen failures and outages, updates will be the responsibility of the user after testing and validating package updates.

The grub password is set via the password_pbkdf2 parameter, however, the rule explicitly checks for GRUB2_PASSWORD. The grub username and password are being set which meets the requirement of the rule, but the check for the rule is not checking for all possible methods of setting a password for a user.
If the parameter is missing from any entries in /boot/loader/entries, the test will fail.
The product will not modify /boot/loader/entries unless the OS version is 9.3 or higher.

The ports that need to be opened from site to site will vary and the product will not make any assumptions on which ports are being used.

The product cannot make assumptions on where organizations are getting their time synchronization data. It will be highly system specific.

This check is incorrectly checking /etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/openssh.config for the values when it should be checking /etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/opensshserver.config. The values being requested by the rule are set in the server file instead of conflicting with the client values being requested in another rule in the openssh.config file.

The values being requested by the rule are set in the server file instead of conflicting with the client values being requested in another rule in the openssh.config file.

The product parses the output of 'sshd -T' for the key that the system uses for communication and ensures that key is appropriately protected. The other keys in that location may not have the correct permissions applied. https://github.com/simp/pupmod-simp-ssh/issues/150 has been created to address this issue.
The UsePrivilegeSeparation ssh server config option was deprecated in openssh version 7.4 the product won't use this parameter unless the openssh version is less than 7.4.
The UsePrivilegeSeparation ssh server config option was deprecated in openssh version 7.4 the product won't use this parameter unless the openssh version is less than 7.4.
The product will cannot enforce this on existing user's passwords, only new ones that are created from the time of remediation.

There is no reliable way to remove extraneous umask commands without causing other errors. However, the umask that the product sets should take precedence over any others in the file since it will be located at the bottom.

All system accounts found will be set to nologin except for the following: - root - sync - shutdown - halt The rule checks at least one of these accounts and flags it as a failure, which is a false negative. It is specifically failing because of the halt and sync accounts. Remediating these accounts could cause loss of access to the system or unexpected system failures.
The product cannot make the distinction between temporary user accounts and permanent ones.
We do not currently have a mechanism for scanning the filesystem for enforcement.
The product cannot determine which accounts are necessary for any given system.
We do not currently have a mechanism for scanning the filesystem for enforcement.

The last login will be displayed as requested, however, the remediation didn't happen in the spot that the rule is checking so this will be a false negative. The lastlogin time will be configured in login_defs.

The scan rule will fail because the module being used currently places a space between the equals sign and the key/value being set in the logind.conf file. There is currently a pull request in the github module being used that should address this issue and prevent future failures - https://github.com/simp/pupmod-voxpupuli-systemd/pull/2.
Users had to make a conscious decision to set "NOPASSWD" in sudoers on a running system. Automatically undoing these settings could negatively impact the system.
This is getting set in the /etc/security/pwquality.conf file, while the check is looking in /etc/pam.d/system-auth. The check should be updated to look in the correct file.
We do not currently have the ability to identify and remediate existing users who do not have this set appropriately, only new users.
Users had to make a conscious decision to set "NOPASSWD" in sudoers on a running system. Automatically undoing these settings could negatively impact the system.
Enforcing this rule will only remove `!authenticate` from the /etc/sudoers file. Any custom files within /etc/sudoers.d will not be modified.
This is getting set in the /etc/sssd/conf.d/50_puppet_service_pam.conf file, while the check is looking in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf. The check should be updated to look in the correct file.

This is getting set in the /etc/sssd/conf.d/99999_puppet_custom.conf file, while the check is looking in /etc/sssd/conf.d/certificate_verification.conf. The check should be updated to look in the correct file.
The product cannot make assumptions about the user's pki certificate structure and will not make assumptions about which certificates are being used and where.
The product cannot make assumptions about the system's pki infrastructure and will not install trust chains automatically.
The product cannot make assumptions about the pki infrastructure of any system it runs on.
This is getting set in the /etc/aide.conf.d/audit_config.aide file, while the check is looking in /etc/aide.conf. The check should be updated to look in the correct file.

This is getting set in the /etc/rsyslog.simp.d/99_simp_local/99_remote_access_methods.conf file, while the check is looking in /etc/rsyslog.conf. The check should be updated to look in the correct file.

The rule wants $ActionSendStreamDriverMode to be set to '1', however, this is the legacy format for setting this parameter. The product will correctly set StreamDriver.Mode to '1' in the imptcp module of /etc/rsyslog.simp.d/00_simp_pre_logging/global.conf, which will achieve the same thing.

The rule wants $ActionSendStreamDriverMode to be set to '1', however, this is the legacy format for setting this parameter. The product will correctly set StreamDriver.Mode to '1' in the imptcp module of /etc/rsyslog.simp.d/00_simp_pre_logging/global.conf, which will achieve the same thing.

The rule wants $DefaultNetstreamDriver to be set to 'gtls', however, this is the legacy format for setting this parameter. The product will correctly set defaultNetstreamDriver to 'gtls' in the global section of /etc/rsyslog.simp.d/00_simp_pre_logging/global.conf, which will achieve the same thing.
The product cannot set a specific remote logging server. The user will need to configure this manually.
We cannot automate deciding how large a weeks worth of logs is. This is a manual process.

The product cannot determine where audit aliases will be stored, the default location will be /etc/aliases, however, if we configure this and users configure the aliases elsewhere there will be no meaningful changes made.

The product will set the mode of the files referenced in this rule to "700" and "600" rather then "750" and "640". This will lock down the files and keep them secure so only admins and ISSMs can access them.

The product will set the mode of the files referenced in this rule to "700" and "600" rather then "750" and "640". This will lock down the files and keep them secure so only admins and ISSMs can access them.

The product cannot determine where audit aliases will be stored, the default location will be /etc/aliases, however, if we configure this and users configure the aliases elsewhere there will be no meaningful changes made.

Turning fips mode on in a machine that isn't already configured for it will cause connection issues to anything not configured to work in fips mode. It is more likely that the user will lose access to their systems or important services than it working out of the box.
Locking a user due to inappropriate encryption is not currently available in simp. A mechanism to address this rule will be implemented in a future release.
There is currently no mechanism to control the content of /etc/ipsec.conf. One may be implemented in the future.

Turning fips mode on in a machine that isn't already configured for it will cause connection issues to anything not configured to work in fips mode. It is more likely that the user will lose access to their systems or important services than it working out of the box.
There is currently no mechanism to control the content of /etc/named.conf. One may be implemented in the future.
This rule requires changes to the BIOS/UEFI settings which the product will never have access to.
This rule requires parameters on users/groups that the product cannot specify because the accounts needed to elevate using sudo will vary between systems.

To avoid loss of access to the system and potential loss of access to certain services, the system's crypto-policy will only be changed to FIPS if the system is FIPS-enabled already. The product does not turn FIPS on at all because it could cause major issues in systems that are unprepared to do so. Turning FIPS on will have to be done by the organization responsible for the managed systems.
